core_meaning: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る means to look at or watch something — it implies directing your gaze intentionally toward something. It covers everything from glancing at your phone to watching a movie, but it always carries a sense of deliberate visual attention.

sentences.jp: ねえ、この<ruby>動画<rt>どうが</rt></ruby><ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>て！めちゃくちゃおもしろいから。
sentences.translation: Hey, watch this video! It's seriously so funny.
sentences.notes: Casual imperative <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>て (dropped ください) is the natural way to tell a friend to look at something.

sentences.jp: <ruby>昨日<rt>きのう</rt></ruby>の<ruby>夜<rt>よる</rt></ruby>、<ruby>映画<rt>えいが</rt></ruby>を<ruby>三本<rt>さんぼん</rt></ruby>も<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>ちゃった。
sentences.translation: I ended up watching three whole movies last night.
sentences.notes: 〜ちゃった adds a nuance of 'oops, I did it without meaning to' — very natural collocation with <ruby>映画<rt>えいが</rt></ruby>を<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る.

sentences.jp: <ruby>子供<rt>こども</rt></ruby>が<ruby>川<rt>かわ</rt></ruby>に<ruby>落<rt>お</rt></ruby>ちるのを<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>て、すぐに<ruby>飛<rt>と</rt></ruby>び<ruby>込<rt>こ</rt></ruby>んだ。
sentences.translation: Seeing the child fall into the river, he immediately jumped in.
sentences.notes: 〜のを<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>て is a key pattern meaning 'upon seeing (something happen)' — <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る takes a nominalized clause as its object here.

sentences.jp: この<ruby>資料<rt>しりょう</rt></ruby>をご<ruby>覧<rt>らん</rt></ruby>ください……あ、すみません、<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>るの<ruby>間違<rt>まちが</rt></ruby>いでした。<ruby>改<rt>あらた</rt></ruby>めて<ruby>確認<rt>かくにん</rt></ruby>してみます。
sentences.translation: Please take a look at this document… ah, sorry, I used the wrong word. I'll check again.
sentences.notes: In formal/polite speech, <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る is replaced by ご<ruby>覧<rt>らん</rt></ruby>になる (honorific) or <ruby>拝見<rt>はいけん</rt></ruby>する (humble) — plain <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る sounds blunt in formal contexts.

sentences.jp: やってみないと<ruby>分<rt>わ</rt></ruby>からないから、とりあえず<ruby>試<rt>ため</rt></ruby>してみよう。
sentences.translation: You won't know until you try, so let's just give it a shot.
sentences.notes: 〜てみる (try doing) is one of the most common grammatical uses of <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る — here it's fully grammaticalized and no longer means 'to look.'

dont_use: Don't use plain <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る in formal situations when talking to or about someone of higher status — use ご<ruby>覧<rt>らん</rt></ruby>になる when the superior is doing the looking, and <ruby>拝見<rt>はいけん</rt></ruby>する when you are. Also, <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る implies intention; if something simply enters your field of vision without you trying, <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える (to be visible, to appear) is more accurate.

confused_with.contrast: A common mistake is using <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る when something is passively visible — for example, saying 「<ruby>山<rt>やま</rt></ruby>を<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る」 when you mean 'the mountain can be seen from here,' which sounds like you're actively staring at it. The natural choice is 「<ruby>山<rt>やま</rt></ruby>が<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える」 — <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える marks something as being visible or in view without implying deliberate effort. Use <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る for intentional watching/looking, and <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える when something is simply perceptible.

confusion_set.use_when: You are actively and intentionally looking at or watching something.
confusion_set.example: <ruby>テレビ<rt>てれび</rt></ruby>を<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>ながらご<ruby>飯<rt>はん</rt></ruby>を<ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べた。

confusion_set.use_when: Something is passively visible or comes into view without deliberate effort.
confusion_set.example: ここから<ruby>富士山<rt>ふじさん</rt></ruby>が<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える。

confusion_set.use_when: You are showing something to someone else — causative/transitive, with a recipient.
confusion_set.example: <ruby>写真<rt>しゃしん</rt></ruby>を<ruby>友達<rt>ともだち</rt></ruby>に<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せた。

frequency: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る is extremely common in daily speech across all registers. It is one of the most frequent verbs in Japanese. Its grammatical form 〜てみる is equally ubiquitous and appears constantly in both spoken and written Japanese.
anki_hint: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る = intentional (you choose to look); <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える = passive (it just appears in your sight) — if you can say 'I watched it,' use <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る; if you'd say 'I could see it,' use <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える.
